option: Many Unit Tests + Fewer Integration Tests + Few E2E Tests
best_for: The general shape to aim for on most projects — fast, cheap unit tests form the base; e2e tests cover only the critical user journeys
avoid_when: A thin CRUD app with almost no business logic to unit-test — there, integration tests covering the API layer directly may give more value per test written
tradeoffs: Fast feedback loop (unit tests run in seconds) with e2e tests reserved for the few flows where only a real browser catches the bug (checkout, auth, payment) — inverting this pyramid (mostly e2e) makes CI slow and flaky

option: Test Business Logic in Isolation From Framework/DB
best_for: Pure functions, service-layer logic, calculations, validation rules — anything that doesn't need a real database or HTTP request to exercise
avoid_when: Code that's mostly framework glue with no real logic of its own — testing that in isolation often just re-tests the framework, not your code
tradeoffs: Fastest possible test feedback (milliseconds, no I/O), and forces business logic out of framework-coupled code into testable functions — a good side effect even beyond the tests themselves

option: Vitest / Jest (Node.js/TypeScript)
best_for: Node.js/TypeScript projects — Vitest for Vite-based projects (faster, ESM-native), Jest for broader ecosystem compatibility and existing Jest-based codebases
avoid_when: A non-JS/TS backend (Python, etc.) — use the stack's native testing framework instead
tradeoffs: Both are mature, fast, and support the same broad feature set (mocking, snapshots, coverage) — Vitest tends to be faster in Vite-based projects specifically due to shared config/transform pipeline

option: pytest (Python)
best_for: FastAPI/Django projects — pytest's fixture system and plugin ecosystem (pytest-asyncio, pytest-django) cover async and framework-specific testing needs well
avoid_when: Never really avoid pytest for Python testing — it's the de facto standard with the broadest ecosystem support
tradeoffs: Fixtures provide clean, composable test setup/teardown; the plugin ecosystem (pytest-cov, pytest-mock, pytest-asyncio) covers nearly every Python testing need without leaving the pytest ecosystem

option: React Testing Library / Vue Testing Library
best_for: Testing components the way a user actually interacts with them (by role, text, label) rather than implementation detail (internal state, class names)
avoid_when: Testing pure presentational logic with no user interaction to simulate — a plain unit test of the underlying function may be simpler
tradeoffs: Encourages accessible markup by design (queries are role/label-based, so an inaccessible component is also a hard-to-test one) and tests survive refactors that don't change user-visible behavior

option: Test the API Layer Against a Real (Test) Database
best_for: Backend API testing — spin up a real test database (often via Docker or an in-memory/ephemeral instance) rather than mocking the DB layer entirely
avoid_when: Pure unit tests of business logic that don't touch the DB at all — mocking there is appropriate and faster
tradeoffs: Catches real query bugs, constraint violations, and ORM/query-builder mistakes that a mocked DB layer would never surface — meaningfully higher confidence than unit tests alone at the cost of slower test runs

option: Supertest (Node.js HTTP assertion testing)
best_for: Express/Nest.js/Fastify APIs — issue real HTTP requests against the app in-process and assert on the response, without needing a running server
avoid_when: GraphQL-specific testing where a GraphQL client-testing library fits the query/mutation shape better
tradeoffs: Tests the actual HTTP layer (status codes, headers, response shape) rather than just the underlying function, catching integration bugs unit tests miss, while still running fast (in-process, no real network)

option: Test Containers for Ephemeral Test Databases
best_for: CI pipelines needing a real, isolated Postgres/MySQL/Redis instance per test run rather than a shared or mocked database
avoid_when: Simple projects where an in-memory SQLite substitute is close enough to production behavior for the tests that matter
tradeoffs: Spins up a real, disposable instance of the actual database engine per test run (via Docker), giving high-fidelity integration tests without a shared, stateful test database that different CI runs could interfere with

option: Playwright
best_for: Cross-browser E2E testing of critical user journeys (signup, checkout, core workflows) — the current best-supported option for multi-browser (Chromium/Firefox/WebKit) automated testing
avoid_when: Testing every possible user flow exhaustively — e2e tests are the most expensive to write and maintain, reserve them for genuinely critical paths (QA001)
tradeoffs: Fast, reliable auto-waiting (reduces flakiness vs manual waits), built-in trace/video capture for debugging failures, and genuine multi-browser support in one API

option: Cypress
best_for: Teams already invested in Cypress's ecosystem, or wanting its component-testing mode alongside e2e in one tool
avoid_when: New projects with no existing Cypress investment — Playwright's multi-browser support and auto-waiting are generally the stronger default choice for a fresh project today
tradeoffs: Strong developer experience (time-travel debugging, live reload) and a mature plugin ecosystem, though multi-browser (especially WebKit/Safari) support has historically lagged Playwright's

option: Only Cover Critical User Journeys, Not Every Page
best_for: Every project — signup, login, checkout/payment, and the core value-delivering action of the product get e2e coverage; a static About page does not
avoid_when: A regulated context requiring documented test coverage of every user-facing flow regardless of criticality — rare, and worth flagging as an explicit exception when it applies
tradeoffs: E2E tests are the slowest and most maintenance-prone tier of the pyramid (QA001) — spending that budget on the flows that actually matter (where a bug means lost revenue or a broken core loop) maximizes ROI per test written

option: Mock External Third-Party Services, Not Your Own Code
best_for: Tests that would otherwise call a real third-party API (Stripe, an email provider, an external data source) — mock the boundary, not your own business logic
avoid_when: Mocking your own database/service layer so thoroughly that the test only verifies the mock was called correctly, not that the real code path works — that's a false-confidence test
tradeoffs: Keeps tests fast and deterministic (no flaky network calls to a real third party) while still exercising your actual code, not a mocked version of it — the boundary to mock is the edge of your system, not the middle of it

option: Factories/Builders for Test Data, Not Hand-Copied Fixtures
best_for: Any test needing realistic entity data — a factory function generating a valid `User`/`Order`/etc. with sensible defaults, overridable per test
avoid_when: A single, genuinely one-off test needing very specific hardcoded values with no reuse value — a literal object may be clearer there than a factory call
tradeoffs: Reduces duplication and makes tests resilient to schema changes (add a new required field once in the factory, not in every test file that constructs that entity) — hand-copied fixture objects break in bulk on any schema change

option: Use Sparingly, for Genuinely Stable Output
best_for: Output that's expected to be stable and where a diff genuinely signals something worth reviewing (e.g. a generated API schema, a stable serialization format)
avoid_when: UI component snapshots that change on every minor style tweak — these become 'update snapshot and move on' rituals that catch nothing real; prefer explicit assertions on specific rendered content instead
tradeoffs: Catches unintended changes to genuinely stable output cheaply, but overused on frequently-changing UI it degrades into a rubber-stamp step nobody actually reviews carefully

option: Contract Tests Between Frontend and Backend (or Between Services)
best_for: Microservices or a frontend/backend split with independent deploy cycles, where a backend change could silently break a frontend expecting the old contract
avoid_when: A tightly coupled monorepo full-stack app (e.g. Next.js) where frontend and backend always deploy together — the contract can't drift since both change atomically
tradeoffs: Catches a breaking API change before it reaches production by verifying both sides against a shared contract definition, valuable specifically when frontend/backend (or two services) can deploy independently of each other

option: Load Test Before a Known Traffic Spike, Not as a Default Habit
best_for: A launch, a marketing campaign, or a known seasonal traffic event where you want confidence the system holds up before it happens for real
avoid_when: Continuous/routine load testing on every PR for a low-traffic internal tool — the setup and run cost isn't justified there
tradeoffs: Surfaces capacity/scaling problems (connection pool exhaustion, missing indexes under concurrent load, rate-limit misconfiguration) before real users do, targeted at the specific events where the cost of finding out live would be highest

option: Fix or Delete Flaky Tests Immediately, Never Let Them Linger
best_for: Any test failing intermittently for reasons unrelated to a real bug (timing, shared state, unmocked network) — fix the root cause or remove the test the same week it's identified as flaky
avoid_when: Never let a flaky test 'just be flaky' long-term — retry-until-pass CI configurations that paper over flakiness quietly erode the entire test suite's signal value
tradeoffs: A test suite where failures are ignored ('oh that one's just flaky') has effectively stopped being a safety net — every flaky test left unaddressed trains the team to ignore future real failures too

option: Track Coverage as a Signal, Not a Target to Game
best_for: Using coverage reports to find genuinely untested critical code paths — that's valuable. Setting a hard numeric threshold (e.g. '90% or the build fails') without judgment about what's actually being tested
avoid_when: A coverage threshold enforced so rigidly that developers write low-value tests (asserting nothing meaningful) purely to hit the number — that's the target being gamed instead of guiding real test quality
tradeoffs: Coverage percentage tells you what code ran during tests, not whether it was tested meaningfully — use it to find gaps in critical logic, not as a proxy for test quality on its own

option: Run Tests in Parallel Across CI Workers
best_for: Test suites large enough that serial execution meaningfully slows the PR feedback loop — split by file or by test-runner's built-in sharding
avoid_when: Small test suites (under a minute or two serially) where parallelization setup overhead isn't worth it yet
tradeoffs: Cuts wall-clock CI time significantly for large suites, at the cost of needing test isolation discipline (tests can't share mutable state) since they may run concurrently on different workers

option: Every Test Independent — No Shared Mutable State or Ordering Dependency
best_for: Every test suite, without exception — a test should pass or fail the same way regardless of what ran before it or in what order
avoid_when: Never applicable to skip — order-dependent tests are one of the most common sources of 'passes locally, fails in CI' (or the reverse) confusion
tradeoffs: A test suite where tests must run in a specific order, or share mutable global/database state, becomes fragile in exactly the ways parallelization (QA019) and re-running individual failing tests both need to not be fragile

option: Next.js — Test Route Handlers and Server Components Separately From Client Components
best_for: Next.js App Router apps: unit/integration test Route Handlers and server-side logic directly (no browser needed); use React Testing Library for client components; reserve Playwright for full-page e2e flows crossing both
avoid_when: Treating every Next.js test as requiring a full browser — most server-side logic can be tested faster and more reliably without one
tradeoffs: Matches each layer to its fastest appropriate test tool rather than defaulting everything to slow, heavier e2e tests just because it's a full-stack framework

option: FastAPI — TestClient for In-Process API Testing
best_for: FastAPI apps: use FastAPI's `TestClient` (built on httpx) to issue requests against the app in-process, without a running server, for both unit-adjacent and integration-style tests
avoid_when: End-to-end browser flows against a full deployed environment — TestClient covers the API layer, not a rendered frontend
tradeoffs: Fast, in-process API testing with the same dependency-injection and validation behavior as production, without the overhead of a real running server per test

option: Django — pytest-django Plus Database Transaction Rollback per Test
best_for: Django/DRF apps: pytest-django wraps each test in a database transaction that rolls back afterward, giving test isolation (QA020) without manually cleaning up data
avoid_when: A test genuinely needing to verify commit/rollback behavior itself — that specific test may need a different isolation strategy
tradeoffs: Each test gets a clean database state automatically without manual teardown code, while still testing against real Django ORM behavior rather than a mock

option: Supabase — Test Against a Local Supabase Instance or a Dedicated Test Project
best_for: Supabase-backed apps: run `supabase start` locally (or use a dedicated Supabase test project/branch) for integration tests exercising real Postgres + RLS policies, not a database mock
avoid_when: Pure unit tests of business logic with no DB/RLS interaction to verify — mock or skip the DB layer entirely there
tradeoffs: RLS policies are part of your authorization logic (see auth-patterns.md) and can't be meaningfully verified against a mock — testing against a real Postgres instance (local or a branch) is the only way to actually confirm a policy behaves as intended

option: Write the Test First for Bug Fixes, Optional Elsewhere
best_for: Fixing a reported bug — write a failing test that reproduces it first, then fix the code until it passes, guaranteeing the bug can't silently regress
avoid_when: Exploratory prototyping where the shape of the solution is still unclear — writing tests against code that will be thrown away or heavily reworked wastes the TDD benefit
tradeoffs: Guarantees every fixed bug gets permanent regression coverage as a side effect of the fix process itself, without requiring full TDD discipline for all new feature development
